comments:
Summary and contributions
This paper studies prioritized planning for multi-robot trajectory coordination. It makes two primary contributions: (1) a revised prioritized planning (RPP) algorithm together with a formal characterization (S>i-avoiding and G<i-avoiding paths) of instances for which the approach is guaranteed to find a solution, and (2) an asynchronous decentralized prioritized planning algorithm (AD-(R)PP) that preserves the centralized guarantees while exploiting distributed compute to speed up convergence. The paper includes termination/soundness proofs for the decentralized variants, experimental comparisons (centralized vs synchronized and asynchronous decentralized variants; PP vs RPP) on real indoor maps and instance classes (free-formed and infrastructure tasks), a comparison against reactive method ORCA, and a hardware-in-the-loop multi-UAV deployment (closed-loop AD-PP). The authors also introduce the notion of a valid infrastructure, motivating practical settings (warehouses, road networks) in which their completeness condition holds.
Strengths
- Useful and practically-motivated theoretical contribution: RPP and the sufficient condition (presence of S>i-avoiding and G<i-avoiding paths for all robots) are clearly stated and proven (constructive proof). The valid infrastructure concept is a nice bridge to real-world settings.
- Asynchronous decentralized algorithm is well motivated, and proofs of termination and soundness are provided. Addressing the synchronized-decentralized inefficiency is important in practice.
- Experiments cover multiple environments, both free-formed and structured/infrastructure scenarios, and include comparison with ORCA and deployment in a mixed-reality multi-UAV setup. The demonstrated speed-up of the asynchronous variant and the coverage improvements of RPP in infrastructure settings are convincing.
- Paper is formal and reasonably clear: definitions, notation, and proofs are presented in a structured way.
Weaknesses and limitations
- Key assumptions limit theoretical guarantees: many proofs and the completeness claim for RPP/AD-RPP assume a complete single-robot planner (Best-traj) and reliable, ordered broadcast communication. In practice single-robot completeness rarely holds on rich dynamic spaces, and communication is often unreliable; the paper partially acknowledges and discusses this in deployment, but the theoretical dependence should be emphasized more and the practical impact quantified.
- Priority assignment: prioritized planning heavily depends on the chosen priority order. The paper does not study methods for selecting or adapting priorities, or quantify sensitivity to priority choices. This is important because poor priority assignments can harm solution quality and coverage.
- Solution quality trade-offs: RPP can produce longer paths (preemptive avoidance of start regions), and decentralized execution can prevent exploitative improvements (replanning condition). These effects are acknowledged and measured (prolongation), but more discussion on practical trade-offs and potential mitigations (e.g., occasional cooperative re-optimization or priority reassignment) would strengthen the contribution.
- Comparisons: experiments compare PP/RPP variants and ORCA (reactive), which is appropriate, but it would be useful to include a coupled (centralized) planner baseline on small instances to quantify optimality loss. Also, more diverse instance distributions and scaling experiments (larger robot counts) would help assess limits.
- Communication/computation complexity: the paper reports number of replannings/messages and wall-clock runtimes (simulated concurrency), but lacks formal or empirical complexity scaling analysis (e.g., with robot count, density of conflicts). That would help practitioners assess applicability at larger scales.
Suggestions for improvement
- Make the assumptions (complete Best-traj, reliable ordered broadcasts) more explicit and discuss the robustness of theoretical claims when these assumptions are relaxed; where possible, provide bounds or empirical sensitivity tests to message loss and planner incompleteness.
- Add experiments exploring sensitivity to priority order and/or simple priority selection heuristics; consider adaptive priority adjustments as a remedy.
- For small n, include a coupled optimal planner baseline (or previously published optimal solutions) to measure cost optimality gap of RPP/PP.
- Provide more analysis (empirical or analytic) of scalability with robot count and conflict density, and comment on expected behavior in denser, highly-coupled cases.
- Discuss possible hybrid strategies (e.g., first run decentralized prioritized planning, then local re-optimization) to improve solution quality while retaining scalability.
Overall assessment and recommendation
This paper provides a valuable and practically-relevant contribution to multi-robot trajectory coordination: a cleanly stated revision of prioritized planning with provable solvable-instance characterization, and a sound asynchronous decentralized implementation that improves runtime in realistic distributed settings. The theoretical results are solid under stated assumptions and the experiments plus UAV deployment support practical usefulness. The limitations (assumptions on planner and communication, priority selection, quality trade-offs) are acknowledged and mostly addressable in future work. I recommend acceptance (strong), with suggested improvements as above.
